verses.text: So king Solomon was king over all Israel. And these were the princes which he had; Azariah the son of Zadok the priest, Elihoreph and Ahiah, the sons of Shisha, scribes; Jehoshaphat the son of Ahilud, the recorder. And Benaiah the son of Jehoiada was over the host: and Zadok and Abiathar were the priests: And Azariah the son of Nathan was over the officers: and Zabud the son of Nathan was principal officer, and the king's friend: And Ahishar was over the household: and Adoniram the son of Abda was over the tribute. Guidance: The cabinet roster of Solomon's reign at its mid-point. The list is the administrative spine of a centralized Near-Eastern kingdom. (1) 'King Solomon was king over all Israel.' The blanket statement of consolidated rule — no rival, no rebellion. (2) Azariah son of Zadok — the chief priest. (Note: this is a different Azariah from Azariah son of Nathan in v5; the Hebrew name is common.) (3) Elihoreph and Ahiah sons of Shisha — the scribes. Two named scribes suggest a substantial royal chancery; their father Shisha is probably the same Sheva/Seraiah who served David (2 Sam 8:17; 20:25); the office is inherited. (4) Jehoshaphat son of Ahilud — the recorder (mazkir — literally 'remembrancer'). The royal historian and protocol officer; the same man who served David (2 Sam 8:16). The chapter is showing continuity from David's administration. (5) Benaiah son of Jehoiada — over the host. The bodyguard commander promoted to commander-in-chief after Joab's execution (1 Kgs 2:35). (6) Zadok and Abiathar 'were the priests' — perhaps a literary inclusion noting both names although Abiathar had been deposed; or the second name refers to Abiathar's son Ahimelech, often confused in the manuscripts. (7) Azariah son of Nathan — over the officers (the twelve district governors of vv7-19). Nathan is probably Nathan the prophet, suggesting Solomon kept the prophet's family in the inner circle. (8) Zabud son of Nathan — 'principal officer, and the king's friend' (re'eh ha-melek — friend of the king). A formal court title; ancient Egyptian and Near-Eastern courts had such 'royal friends.' Hushai had filled the role under David (2 Sam 15:37). (9) Ahishar — over the household (al ha-bayit — palace steward). (10) Adoniram son of Abda — over the tribute (ha-mas — corvée labor, conscripted work-gangs). The same Adoram who served David (2 Sam 20:24); the labor-conscription office is critical and ultimately controversial — when Adoram tries to collect tribute under Rehoboam, the people will stone him (1 Kgs 12:18). The chapter is preserving the institutional spine of the kingdom: priest, scribes, recorder, general, priest, governor-of-governors, king's friend, palace steward, corvée master. The kingdom is a working bureaucracy, not just a royal personality.

And Solomon had twelve officers over all Israel, which provided victuals for the king and his household: each man his month in a year made provision. And these are their names: The son of Hur, in mount Ephraim: The son of Dekar, in Makaz, and in Shaalbim, and Bethshemesh, and Elonbethhanan: The son of Hesed, in Aruboth; to him pertained Sochoh, and all the land of Hepher: The son of Abinadab, in all the region of Dor; which had Taphath the daughter of Solomon to wife: Baana the son of Ahilud; to him pertained Taanach and Megiddo, and all Bethshean, which is by Zartanah beneath Jezreel, from Bethshean to Abelmeholah, even unto the place that is beyond Jokneam: The son of Geber, in Ramothgilead; to him pertained the towns of Jair the son of Manasseh, which are in Gilead; to him also pertained the region of Argob, which is in Bashan, threescore great cities with walls and brasen bars: Ahinadab the son of Iddo had Mahanaim: Ahimaaz was in Naphtali; he also took Basmath the daughter of Solomon to wife: Baanah the son of Hushai was in Asher and in Aloth: Jehoshaphat the son of Paruah, in Issachar: Shimei the son of Elah, in Benjamin: Geber the son of Uri was in the country of Gilead, in the country of Sihon king of the Amorites, and of Og king of Bashan; and he was the only officer which was in the land. Guidance: The twelve provincial administrators — Solomon's reorganization of Israel for tax-and-provision purposes. The crucial detail: 'each man his month in a year made provision.' Solomon has divided the kingdom into twelve administrative districts, each responsible for feeding the royal court for one month a year on a rotating schedule. The system bypasses the old tribal boundaries (most of the districts don't match the twelve tribes) — instead the boundaries are drawn for administrative efficiency. This is centralization. Note the political bonds: two of the twelve governors are Solomon's sons-in-law (the son of Abinadab over Dor married Taphath; Ahimaaz over Naphtali married Basmath). Marriage alliances knit governors to the royal family. Several governors are sons of David's officers — Baana son of Ahilud (his father was the recorder), Baanah son of Hushai (David's friend who outwitted Ahithophel) — the same families serve both kings. The geography ranges across all the tribal territories: Mount Ephraim, the lowland villages of Dan-Benjamin, the Sharon plain, the Jezreel valley, the Galilee, Transjordan (Mahanaim, Gilead, Bashan with its sixty walled cities). The list testifies to Solomon's effective control over the whole expanded kingdom. The chapter is showing us efficient government — but is also planting a seed of trouble. The rotating-provision system functioned as taxation. The twelve districts' burden of feeding the royal court fed into the long-term complaint that later breaks out under Rehoboam: 'thy father made our yoke grievous' (1 Kgs 12:4). The kingdom looks magnificent at this stage; the seeds of its breakup are quietly being sown in the corvée and the food-tax.

Judah and Israel were many, as the sand which is by the sea in multitude, eating and drinking, and making merry. And Solomon reigned over all kingdoms from the river unto the land of the Philistines, and unto the border of Egypt: they brought presents, and served Solomon all the days of his life. Guidance: The two summary lines — domestic and international. (1) Population: 'Judah and Israel were many, as the sand which is by the sea in multitude.' The patriarchal promises to Abraham (Gen 22:17) and Jacob (Gen 32:12) were that their descendants would be as the sand of the sea; here, in Solomon's day, the narrator says the promise is visibly fulfilled. The Israelites are populous; the people are in a phase of demographic growth. 'Eating and drinking, and making merry' — the standard biblical idiom for prosperity. Compare Mic 4:4 — every man under his vine and fig tree; the chapter is showing the high-water mark of Israel's national flourishing. (2) Empire: 'Solomon reigned over all kingdoms from the river unto the land of the Philistines, and unto the border of Egypt.' The 'river' is the Euphrates. The Solomonic empire, at its peak, extended from the Euphrates in the northeast down through Aram and Transjordan, across the Jordan to Israel proper, southwest to the border of Egypt at the Wadi-el-Arish. This is the geographical extent promised to Abraham in Gen 15:18 — 'from the river of Egypt unto the great river, the river Euphrates.' Solomon's empire is the only time in Israel's history this extent is actually held. 'They brought presents, and served Solomon.' The surrounding kingdoms paid annual tribute. The chapter is showing us the apex of the Israelite empire. The kingdom Christians look for in Christ is presented in Solomon's reign as a historical typology — the LORD's anointed king at peace, the patriarchal promises visibly fulfilled, the nations bringing tribute.

And Solomon's provision for one day was thirty measures of fine flour, and threescore measures of meal, Ten fat oxen, and twenty oxen out of the pastures, and an hundred sheep, beside harts, and roebucks, and fallowdeer, and fatted fowl. Guidance: The daily palace provisions. The figures: 30 kor of fine flour + 60 kor of meal (a kor was about 6 bushels; so roughly 195 bushels of fine flour and 390 bushels of regular meal per day). 10 stall-fed oxen + 20 grass-fed oxen + 100 sheep, plus venison from harts (probably red deer), roebucks (smaller deer), fallow deer (or possibly gazelles), and fattened poultry. Estimates suggest this fed a court of around 14,000-32,000 people daily, depending on the exact size of the kor measure and the proportions consumed. The chapter is giving us numbers because numbers convey grandeur. The Solomonic court was not a small royal household; it was a major institution — government officers, foreign emissaries, military commanders, religious officials, household staff, family members, retainers, guests. The daily provision lists the king's expense and, more pointedly, the country's expense in supporting him. The twelve districts of vv7-19 were each producing this monthly. The chapter is honest about both the scale of the prosperity and the scale of the consumption. Bread and meat poured into the palace daily; the nation's surplus, gathered through twelve administrative districts, was concentrated in Jerusalem.

For he had dominion over all the region on this side the river, from Tiphsah even to Azzah, over all the kings on this side the river: and he had peace on all sides round about him. And Judah and Israel dwelt safely, every man under his vine and under his fig tree, from Dan even to Beersheba, all the days of Solomon. Guidance: Peace and security — the most evocative lines in the chapter. 'He had dominion… from Tiphsah even to Azzah.' Tiphsah is identified with Thapsacus on the Euphrates (in modern Syria); Azzah is Gaza, the Philistine city near the Egyptian border. The dimension is from the northeast frontier to the southwest frontier of the empire. 'He had peace on all sides round about him.' Hebrew shalom — the comprehensive Hebrew word for wholeness, security, well-being. No active war on any frontier. Then the famous image: 'Judah and Israel dwelt safely, every man under his vine and under his fig tree, from Dan even to Beersheba.' This is one of the foundational images of Israelite eschatology. The combination 'vine and fig tree' was the standard agricultural ideal — a man's own plot, his own vines, his own fruit tree, his own family. The image will recur in Micah 4:4 and Zechariah 3:10 as the picture of messianic peace. Solomon's reign was, in a historical sense, the realized version of what later prophets would paint as future hope. From Dan in the far north to Beersheba in the far south — the full extent of Israel proper — every man sat under his own vine and fig tree without fear. The chapter is showing us a snapshot of life under a wise king at peace with neighbors. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's intended life for His covenant people is not constant warfare but rooted, peaceful flourishing in family and field. That this picture appears in 1 Kings 4 is a foretaste of the picture Christians believe will appear permanently when the eternal Davidic King reigns.

And Solomon had forty thousand stalls of horses for his chariots, and twelve thousand horsemen. And those officers provided victual for king Solomon, and for all that came unto king Solomon's table, every man in his month: they lacked nothing. Barley also and straw for the horses and dromedaries brought they unto the place where the officers were, every man according to his charge. Guidance: The military establishment and its supply. The numbers: 40,000 stalls (urvot — stalls or stables) for chariot horses, plus 12,000 horsemen (parashim — cavalry, or possibly chariot crews). The Hebrew text of 1 Kings has 40,000; the parallel passage in 2 Chr 9:25 has 4,000, which is probably the original — likely a copying error where the Hebrew word for thousand was duplicated. Even 4,000 chariot-stalls and 12,000 horsemen represents a substantial militarized state. The chapter is interestingly ambivalent. Israel's law in Deuteronomy 17:16 had warned the king specifically about multiplying horses: 'he shall not multiply horses to himself, nor cause the people to return to Egypt, to the end that he should multiply horses.' Solomon's chariot-arsenal sits in direct tension with the deuteronomic prohibition. The narrator records it without explicit comment — but the careful reader is meant to notice. The provisioning detail: 'they lacked nothing.' Hebrew lo ye'adru davar — they did not lack a thing. The administrative system worked. 'Barley also and straw for the horses and dromedaries.' Even animal fodder was supplied on schedule. The chapter is showing us a flourishing military-industrial state that is also, by Mosaic-law standards, drifting toward an idolatry of military might. The next several chapters will continue the same pattern: success that also violates the warnings of Deut 17. Solomon's reign is at once the historical apex of Israelite power and the historical seedbed of Israel's later spiritual decline.

And God gave Solomon wisdom and understanding exceeding much, and largeness of heart, even as the sand that is on the sea shore. And Solomon's wisdom excelled the wisdom of all the children of the east country, and all the wisdom of Egypt. For he was wiser than all men; than Ethan the Ezrahite, and Heman, and Chalcol, and Darda, the sons of Mahol: and his fame was in all nations round about. Guidance: The chapter pivots to wisdom — back to the gift granted at Gibeon. 'God gave Solomon wisdom and understanding exceeding much, and largeness of heart, even as the sand that is on the sea shore.' The same sand-of-the-sea image used for the population (v20) is now applied to Solomon's wisdom. The thought is staggering: as the LORD has multiplied the people, He has multiplied the wisdom of their king. The Hebrew rochav lev — 'breadth of heart' — is a beautiful idiom for capacious-mindedness. Solomon's intellect can hold large amounts of information; his moral discernment can encompass many issues at once. Then the international comparison: Solomon excels the wisdom of (1) 'all the children of the east country' (the bene-kedem — Edomite, Arabian, and Mesopotamian sages, famous for proverbial wisdom; compare Job and his friends, all easterners) and (2) 'all the wisdom of Egypt' (the Egyptian sapiential tradition, which produced wisdom literature like the Instructions of Amenemope, with parallels to Proverbs 22). Solomon outshines both of the great ancient near-eastern wisdom traditions. Then four named domestic sages: Ethan the Ezrahite (probably the author of Psalm 89), Heman (the chief temple musician of 1 Chr 6:33, also possibly the author of Psalm 88), Chalcol, and Darda — the sons of Mahol (possibly a Levitical guild of musicians-poets). Solomon is wiser than the four most respected wise men of his own day. The chapter is establishing Solomon's wisdom as not merely greater than his peers but a different scale of greater. His fame spread to 'all nations round about' — international reputation. The chapter is setting up the visit of the Queen of Sheba in chapter 10; word of his wisdom traveled far enough that foreign royalty came to verify it.

And he spake three thousand proverbs: and his songs were a thousand and five. And he spake of trees, from the cedar tree that is in Lebanon even unto the hyssop that springeth out of the wall: he spake also of beasts, and of fowl, and of creeping things, and of fishes. And there came of all people to hear the wisdom of Solomon, from all kings of the earth, which had heard of his wisdom. Guidance: The output and the audience of Solomon's wisdom. (1) The numbers. 3000 proverbs (a fraction of which survive in the canonical book of Proverbs, especially chapters 10-22 and 25-29 attributed to Solomon). 1005 songs. Of his songs only the Song of Solomon and the psalms attributed to him (72 and 127) survive in the canonical text. The numbers indicate that Solomon was a prolific literary producer — what survives in the Bible is a curated selection from a much larger Solomonic literature. (2) The natural-history range. 'He spake of trees, from the cedar tree that is in Lebanon even unto the hyssop that springeth out of the wall.' The cedar is the tallest, mightiest tree of the region; the hyssop is the smallest plant — a low aromatic herb that grows in crevices of walls. The merism (largest to smallest) is the Hebrew way of saying 'every plant in between.' Solomon studied the whole flora. 'He spake also of beasts, and of fowl, and of creeping things, and of fishes' — every category of animal life mentioned in the creation account of Genesis 1. The chapter is portraying Solomon as a kind of biblical Aristotle — a natural philosopher who classified the world. None of his nature-treatises survive in canonical form; the book of Ecclesiastes, attributed to Solomon, contains some natural-philosophical reflection but is not technical biology. (3) The audience. 'There came of all people to hear the wisdom of Solomon, from all kings of the earth.' His court became an international wisdom-school. Foreign kings sent embassies; foreign sages came to learn. The chapter is ending on the note of cosmopolitan grandeur. Solomon's wisdom is not parochial; it has international standing. The chapter is preparing us for the next phase of the narrative — the building of the temple. Solomon will use this wisdom, these political alliances, this prosperous economy, this stable military, this international reputation, to construct the great house of the LORD on Mount Moriah. The chapter has set the table for chapter 5's preparations and chapter 6's construction.
